title: Promises and chains

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Promises and chains; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Promises and chains
sources.claim: A Promise represents eventual fulfillment or rejection, and then creates a new promise whose result follows its handler.

sources.locator: MDN JavaScript Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Promises and chains
sources.claim: A Promise represents eventual fulfillment or rejection, and then creates a new promise whose result follows its handler.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Promise represents eventual fulfillment or rejection, and then creates a new promise whose result follows its handler.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A parse step thrown inside then rejects the next promise and is handled by a terminal catch. Your goal is to trace and implement a promise chain with correctly propagated failures. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Call stack and event loop model; Closures and lifetime. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: A Promise represents eventual fulfillment or rejection, and then creates a new promise whose result follows its handler. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Errors can become unhandled, executors run synchronously, and Promise.all fails fast while work may continue. This draft therefore rejects: then mutates and returns the same Promise object.

includes: Represent eventual completion and compose fulfillment, rejection and cleanup without floating promises.
Trace and implement a promise chain with correctly propagated failures.
excludes: Errors can become unhandled, executors run synchronously, and Promise.all fails fast while work may continue.

outcomes: Trace and implement a promise chain with correctly propagated failures.

examples.title: Promises and chains: concrete case
examples.situation: A parse step thrown inside then rejects the next promise and is handled by a terminal catch.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A parse step thrown inside then rejects the next promise and is handled by a terminal catch.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: then returns a new promise, enabling transformation and error propagation through a chain.
examples.result: The result follows because then returns a new promise, enabling transformation and error propagation through a chain.

examples.title: Promises and chains: boundary case
examples.situation: Errors can become unhandled, executors run synchronously, and Promise.all fails fast while work may continue.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Errors can become unhandled, executors run synchronously, and Promise.all fails fast while work may continue.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: then mutates and returns the same Promise object.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: then returns a new promise, enabling transformation and error propagation through a chain.

analogy.example: A coordinated event desk can model Promises and chains, with values, tasks, callbacks, and external messages changing observable state.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Promises and chains before using exact platform terms
limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.

recap.summary: You should now be able to trace and implement a promise chain with correctly propagated failures. Keep this boundary in view: Errors can become unhandled, executors run synchronously, and Promise.all fails fast while work may continue.

Why is this claim incorrect: then mutates and returns the same Promise object.
Apply checkpoint 'new promise' to a new artifact.
